_comment: Category-level prerequisite edges for the math system adaptive diagnostic. from_category_id failing implies likely deficit in to_category_id. strength 0-1. Category ids match design-spec §Categories (19).

edges.note: Signed-number arithmetic and rational-number fluency are the numeric substrate for all algebraic manipulation
Multi-step equation solving requires signed-number and fraction arithmetic at each step
Exponent laws require fluent rational-number and negative-number arithmetic
Rate-of-change arithmetic in 1.1–1.3 requires clean real-number operations
Arithmetic/geometric sequence recognition (2.1–2.2) requires percent, ratio, and proportional reasoning
Exact trig values at special angles require rational-number and radical arithmetic
Distribution and equivalent-expression recognition are the manipulation engine for multi-step linear solving
Equivalent expression manipulation underpins substitution and elimination setup
Polynomial arithmetic (addition, distribution) is a direct extension of algebraic-expression manipulation
Equivalent-form rewriting (1.11) and transformation construction (1.12) require fluent distribution and substitution
Log manipulation (2.12) and exponential manipulation (2.4) require distribution and equivalent-expression skills
Trig identity work (3.12) and symbolic FRQ 4 require distribution and equivalent expression fluency
Systems require solving individual linear equations as a sub-step; literal-equation fluency enables parameter extraction
Interval notation and inequality solutions are the language of domain and range
Solving exponential/log equations (2.13) and trig equations (3.10) mirrors multi-step linear algebra; literal-equation fluency enables parameter extraction in function models
Solving exponential and logarithmic equations (2.13) is multi-step linear solving extended to non-linear forms
Solving trig equations (3.10) requires the same inverse-operation reasoning as linear equation solving
Constructing polynomial/exponential models from two conditions (1.14, 2.14) sometimes requires solving a 2×2 system
Two-point exponential and logarithmic modeling (2.5, 2.14) may require solving a 2×2 system for parameters
Sinusoidal model construction from two known points (3.7) sometimes reduces to a system of equations
AP Precalc Unit 1 is applied polynomial algebra; factoring, zeros, end behavior, and rational simplification all require polynomial fluency
Composition of functions (2.7) uses polynomial-style reasoning; algebraic manipulation for 2.4 requires polynomial fluency
Algebraic limit manipulation (factoring, cancellation) requires polynomial factoring fluency
Differentiating and simplifying polynomial expressions is the base case for all derivative rules
Rational exponents appear in polynomial zero work (1.5) and equivalent-representation questions
Exponent laws are the operational core of Unit 2; rational exponents connect to 2.3–2.4; radical structure underpins exact trig values
Exact trig values (3.3) require radical simplification; trig identity proofs use algebraic manipulation involving radicals
Power rule with negative and fractional exponents requires rational-exponent fluency from F6
Antiderivative of x^n with rational exponents requires exponent-law fluency; reverse power rule is an exponent operation
Function notation, domain/range, piecewise evaluation, and graph reading are assumed throughout Unit 1
Composition (2.7) and inverse functions (2.8) require function-notation fluency and graph interpretation
Reading sinusoidal graphs, identifying amplitude/period/midline, and evaluating trig at inputs all require function-notation and graph fluency
Parametric functions (4.1–4.4) are an extension of function notation with two component functions
Limits of piecewise and rational functions require evaluating functions at boundary points and reading graph behavior
Derivative definition as a limit requires function-notation fluency; secant line construction requires evaluating f at two inputs
Limits of rational functions (factoring, holes, vertical asymptotes) require P1 rational-function algebra
Polynomial derivative computation and rational function differentiation (quotient rule) require P1 fluency
Sign-chart analysis for polynomial expressions (zeros, multiplicity) is the engine for increase/decrease and concavity analysis
Derivatives of e^x and ln(x) build directly on exponential/log function structure from P2
Antiderivative of e^x and 1/x require recognizing exponential and logarithmic structures from P2
Exponential growth/decay DE solution (dy/dt = ky → y = Ce^(kt)) requires P2 exponential-function fluency
Limits involving trig functions (especially sin x/x squeeze) require trig-value fluency from P3
Derivatives of sin, cos, tan require knowing the trig function definitions and identities from P3
Derivatives of inverse trig functions (arcsin, arctan) and trig chain-rule applications require P3 inverse-trig and trig identity fluency
Antiderivatives of trig functions (cos → sin, sec² → tan) require P3 trig derivative knowledge in reverse
Derivative definition is a limit; differentiability requires continuity; all of Unit 2 presupposes Unit 1 limit fluency
Chain rule (3.1) extends basic derivative rules; implicit differentiation requires fluent application of all Unit 2 rules
All contextual applications require derivative computation including chain, product, and quotient rules from Unit 2
Related rates (4.4–4.5) use implicit differentiation; L'Hopital (4.7) uses derivatives of composite forms
First and second derivative tests require fluent derivative computation from Unit 2
Implicit curve analysis (5.12) requires implicit differentiation; higher-order derivatives require chain rule
MVT and EVT (5.1–5.2) build on rate-of-change interpretation established in Unit 4
FTC connects limits (Riemann sum limit) to the integral; antiderivative definition uses limit notation from Unit 1
Antiderivatives are inverse derivative operations; fluent differentiation from Unit 2 is required to find them
U-substitution (6.9) is the chain rule reversed; FTC Part 1 with composite upper limits requires chain rule
Setting up differential equations requires interpreting dy/dx as a derivative from Unit 2
Separation of variables uses chain rule in reverse; exponential DEs involve d/dx(e^(kt)) = ke^(kt)
Separation of variables requires integrating both sides; particular solution requires applying FTC Part 2
Particle motion (8.2) is the integration counterpart to Unit 4 velocity/acceleration analysis
Area-between-curves requires sign-chart reasoning to identify where f > g; optimization setup mirrors Unit 5 candidates test
Every Unit 8 topic (area, volume, average value, particle motion) uses definite integral evaluation from Unit 6
